And it tests whether you know **why** the age rule exists, not just that it does.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 2 — A 10-month-old boy has had three episodes of febrile urinary tract infection. Ultrasound shows a dilated left ureter and a thick-walled bladder. What investigation is indicated, why, and what complication are you trying to prevent?
cards.lecturer:
**Investigation of choice: VOIDING CYSTO-URETHROGRAPHY (VCUG).**

**Basis:** VCUG is the **definitive method for demonstrating the lower urinary tract**, and the **gold standard for diagnosing and grading VUR and PUV obstruction**. Two of his findings are explicit indications: **suspected ureteric dilatation**, and a **thick walled bladder seen by US**. Contrast is injected into the bladder to the upper tract, and **films are taken during instillation, voiding and post-voiding**.

**Also indicated:** a **static renal scan** — for **detection of renal scars**, and in the **acute phase of pyelonephritis to demonstrate parenchymal involvement**. A **dynamic renogram (Tc-DTPA or Tc-MAG3)** may be used for **determining differential renal function**.

**Supporting investigations:** urine m/c/s by an appropriate collection method — noting that **SPA is most reliable and useful in children less than 2 years**, where **any growth is significant** because bladder urine should be sterile; urinalysis; serum electrolytes, urea and creatinine; blood pressure measurement.

**Complication being prevented: RENAL SCARRING**, which leads to **hypertension and chronic kidney disease**.

**Demerits of VCUG to counsel about:** **high dose of radiation** and **risk of UTI**.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Read the ultrasound findings as questions, not answers

---

**"Dilated left ureter"** → **why** is it dilated?

**"Thick-walled bladder"** → **why** is the bladder muscle hypertrophied?

---

>> **A bladder wall thickens because it has been working against resistance** — like any muscle that trains against a load.

**That suggests either obstruction or dysfunctional voiding.**

---

>> **Ultrasound has told you something is wrong. It cannot tell you what.**

**This is the structure-versus-function limitation again.**

---

## Step 2: Both findings are named VCUG indications

Go to the list:

>> **Suspected ureteric dilatation** ✓

>> **Thick walled bladder seen by US** ✓

---

**Two of the six indications, in one child.**

---

## Step 3: What is VUR, and why does the ureter dilate?

>> **VUR = vesicoureteric reflux** — urine flows **BACKWARDS** from bladder to ureter and kidney.

---

**Why does that happen?**

**Step 1:** Normally the ureter enters the bladder at an **oblique angle**, tunnelling through the wall

**Step 2:** As the bladder fills, that tunnel is **compressed shut** — a one-way flap valve

**Step 3:** If the tunnel is too **short or straight**, the valve fails

**Step 4:** Urine is pushed **back up** during voiding, when bladder pressure is highest

**Step 5:** The ureter **dilates** under the repeated back-pressure

---

>> **The dilated ureter is the footprint of urine going the wrong way.**

---

## Step 4: Why is the VOIDING part of the test essential?

>> **"Films are taken during INSTILLATION, VOIDING and POST-VOIDING."**

---

**Reflux happens when bladder pressure is HIGHEST — which is during VOIDING.**

---

>> **A study that fills the bladder but does not film the child voiding can completely miss the diagnosis.**

**That is why it is called a VOIDING cystourethrogram.** The name is the method.

---

**And in a boy, the voiding films also show the urethra** — revealing **posterior urethral valves (PUV)** as a dilated posterior urethra above an obstruction.

>> **In a 10-month-old BOY with a thick-walled bladder, PUV must be excluded.** VCUG is the gold standard for that too.

---

## Step 5: The complication you are racing to prevent

>> **RENAL SCARRING.**

---

**Follow the chain:**

**Step 1:** VUR pushes infected urine **up to the kidney**

**Step 2:** The kidney becomes infected — **pyelonephritis**

**Step 3:** Each episode of pyelonephritis **destroys some renal tissue**

**Step 4:** Destroyed tissue heals as a **SCAR**

**Step 5:** Scars are **permanent** — dead, fibrous, non-functioning

**Step 6:** Enough scarring → **HYPERTENSION and CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE**

---

>> **He has already had THREE febrile UTIs. Each one may have cost him kidney tissue.**

---

>> **This is why finding VUR in a baby matters so much: you are preventing adult hypertension and renal failure, decades ahead.**

**And note that hypertension appears in this lecture as both a risk factor for and a feature of renal disease — this is how that circle begins.**

---

## Step 6: How do you find out whether scarring has ALREADY happened?

>> **STATIC RENAL SCAN** — its listed uses include **detection of renal scars** and the **acute phase of pyelonephritis to demonstrate parenchymal involvement**.

---

**Why does it work?**

**Step 1:** The tracer is taken up by **functioning tubular cells**

**Step 2:** A scar has **no functioning cells**

**Step 3:** It takes up **no tracer**

**Step 4:** It shows as a **cold defect** — a hole in the picture

---

>> **Compare with IVU, which has "LOW SENSITIVITY for renal scar detection."** For scars, the static scan is decisively better.

---

**And if you need to know how much function each kidney still has** → **dynamic renography (DTPA or MAG3)** for **differential renal function**.

---

## Step 7: The urine sample — how should it be taken at this age?

**He is 10 months old.**

---

>> **"SPA — MOST RELIABLE, useful in children less than 2 years."**

**And with SPA, ANY growth is significant, because bladder urine should be sterile.**

---

>> **In a baby, a bag specimen is nearly worthless when positive** — high contamination risk. It is useful only for its **negative predictive value**.

**Given three previous febrile UTIs, you need a sample you can actually trust.**

---

## Step 8: What must you warn the family about?

>> **HIGH DOSE OF RADIATION** — and this is a baby, with a long life ahead

>> **RISK OF UTI** — a catheter is passed into the bladder to instil the contrast, carrying organisms into a sterile space

---

**There is a real irony here:** you are investigating recurrent UTI with a test that can cause a UTI.

>> **That is exactly why the indications are specific.** You do not do a VCUG on every child with one infection — you do it when the ultrasound has already raised the question.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Recurrent febrile UTI + dilated ureter + thick-walled bladder = suspect VUR, and in a boy, PUV.**

>> **VCUG is the GOLD STANDARD — and the VOIDING films are the ones that make the diagnosis.**

>> **Static renal scan looks for scars that have already formed.**

>> **And the whole point is to prevent hypertension and chronic kidney disease in twenty years' time.**
cards.tie:
This case shows the investigations working in sequence:

**Ultrasound raises the question · VCUG answers it · the isotope scan measures the damage.**

Each test does the one thing the others cannot.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 3 — A 7-year-old girl with severe gastroenteritis has passed very little urine for 24 hours. Her creatinine has risen from 0.4 to 1.6 mg/dL. Her urine sodium is low. How do you determine whether this is pre-renal AKI or established acute tubular necrosis, and why does it matter?
cards.lecturer:
**The distinguishing test is the FRACTIONAL EXCRETION OF SODIUM (FENa).**

**Basis:** extracellular fluid volume normally determines the urinary sodium excretion. **FENa is equal to or less than 1 in normovolaemia and PRE-RENAL AKI**, and **greater than 1 in impaired tubular transport, e.g. ATN**.

**FENa (%) = (UNa × Pcr) / (PNa × Ucr) × 100**

**Her low urine sodium suggests the tubules are still avidly reabsorbing sodium — that is, a pre-renal picture.**

**Why it matters:** in **pre-renal AKI** the kidney is structurally intact and hypoperfused, so **volume replacement restores renal function**. In **ATN** the tubules are damaged, and aggressive fluid loading will not restore function but will cause fluid overload, pulmonary oedema and hypertension.

**Other investigations:** serum electrolytes, urea and creatinine — noting that **urea is reabsorbed by the renal tubules and its plasma level is affected by state of hydration**, so urea rises disproportionately in pre-renal states; **arterial blood gases** for acidosis; full blood count; urinalysis and urine microscopy for casts; **abdominal USS KUB** to exclude obstruction; and **GFR estimation using the Schwartz formula** — GFR = 0.413 × height (cm) / serum creatinine (mg/dL).

**Note:** renal biopsy is indicated in **unexplained AKI when pre- and post-renal causes are excluded**, or in **persisting ATN when the cause remains uncertain after complete evaluation**.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The clinical problem — two conditions that look identical

---

**She has oliguria and a rising creatinine. There are two possibilities:**

---

**PRE-RENAL AKI** — the kidney is **healthy** but starved of blood.

> She has severe gastroenteritis. She has lost fluid. Her circulating volume is low. Renal perfusion falls.

**The kidney is doing nothing wrong.**

---

**ATN (acute tubular necrosis)** — the kidney tissue is now **damaged**.

> Prolonged hypoperfusion eventually **kills** tubular cells.

---

>> **Pre-renal AKI, if not corrected, BECOMES ATN.** They are two points on the same road.

**Your job is to work out where on that road she is.**

---

## Step 2: Why can you not tell them apart clinically?

Both give:

- Oliguria
- Rising creatinine
- A dehydrated-looking child

---

>> **You cannot distinguish them by looking. You need a test of TUBULAR FUNCTION.**

---

## Step 3: The reasoning behind FENa — think like a kidney

---

**Ask: what SHOULD a healthy kidney do when the body is volume-depleted?**

**Step 1:** It senses low perfusion

**Step 2:** The correct response is to **conserve salt and water**

**Step 3:** Sodium **holds water with it** — retaining sodium restores volume

**Step 4:** So the tubules reabsorb **almost all** the filtered sodium

**Step 5:** Very little sodium appears in the urine

**Step 6:** **FENa ≤ 1**

---

>> **A LOW FENa is GOOD NEWS. It means the tubules are alive and working hard.**

**The kidney is not the problem. It is responding correctly to a problem elsewhere.**

---

**Now the damaged kidney:**

**Step 1:** In ATN, tubular cells are **injured**

**Step 2:** Sodium reabsorption is an **active, energy-requiring** process

**Step 3:** Damaged cells **cannot perform it**

**Step 4:** Sodium **leaks through** into the urine

**Step 5:** **FENa > 1**

---

>> **A HIGH FENa means the tubules have FAILED.**

---

## Step 4: What her low urine sodium already tells you

>> **Low urine sodium = the tubules are gripping sodium tightly = they are still working.**

**That points to PRE-RENAL.**

---

>> But **calculate the FENa properly** rather than relying on urine sodium alone — because urine sodium concentration is affected by how **concentrated** the urine is, whereas FENa corrects for that using creatinine.

---

## Step 5: Why does the distinction change management so completely?

---

**If PRE-RENAL (FENa ≤ 1):**

>> **Give fluid.** The kidney is intact; restore the volume and function returns, often within hours.

---

**If ATN (FENa > 1):**

>> **Do NOT fluid-load aggressively.** The tubules are damaged; fluid will not repair them.

**What you will produce instead:**

- **Fluid overload**
- **Pulmonary oedema**
- **Hypertension**

---

>> **Same child, same appearance, opposite treatment. And getting it wrong drowns her.**

**This is why FENa earns its place in a lecture that could not cover everything.**

---

## Step 6: The supporting clue — the urea-to-creatinine relationship

Go back to the GFR card:

>> **"Urea is REABSORBED by the renal tubules"** and its plasma level is **"affected by state of HYDRATION."**

---

**In pre-renal AKI:**

**Step 1:** The kidney reabsorbs water avidly to conserve volume

**Step 2:** **Urea is dragged back with the water**

**Step 3:** So urea rises **more** than creatinine

---

>> **A disproportionately raised urea supports a PRE-RENAL picture.**

**The very property that makes urea a poor GFR marker makes it a useful clue here.**

---

## Step 7: Interpret her creatinine properly

>> **0.4 → 1.6 mg/dL. That is a FOUR-FOLD rise.**

---

**Apply the Schwartz formula:**

>> **GFR = 0.413 × height (cm) ÷ serum creatinine**

**Creatinine is on the bottom.** Quadruple it, and the calculated GFR falls to **a quarter**.

---

>> **Her kidney function has fallen to roughly 25% of baseline.**

**And remember from the GFR card: creatinine is SECRETED, so it FLATTERS the kidney.** The true fall may be worse.

---

## Step 8: What else must you exclude?

---

>> **POST-RENAL causes — obstruction.**

**AKI is classified as pre-renal, renal or post-renal, and you must exclude all three.**

**Order an abdominal USS KUB** — looking for hydronephrosis and a full bladder. Quick, cheap, no radiation.

---

**Also:**

- **Electrolytes** — especially **potassium**, which is life-threatening in AKI
- **Arterial blood gases** — for **acidosis**, since the kidney maintains acid-base homeostasis
- **Urine microscopy** — **muddy brown granular casts** suggest ATN
- **FBC**

---

## Step 9: When would a biopsy be needed?

>> **"Unexplained AKI — when pre- and post-renal causes are EXCLUDED."**

>> **"PERSISTING ATN when the cause remains uncertain after complete evaluation."**

---

**Not now.** She has an obvious explanation — severe gastroenteritis with volume depletion.

>> **Biopsy only if she does not behave as expected** — the same principle as the nephrotic and AGN indications.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Pre-renal and ATN look identical clinically — you need a test of TUBULAR function.**

>> **FENa ≤ 1 = tubules working = PRE-RENAL = give fluid.**

>> **FENa > 1 = tubules failed = ATN = do not fluid-load.**

>> **A disproportionately high urea supports pre-renal — and always exclude obstruction with an ultrasound.**
cards.tie:
This case shows why the lecture separates **GFR estimation** from **tubular function assessment**.

GFR tells you **how badly** the kidney is failing.

FENa tells you **why** — and therefore what to do about it.

cards.q: KEY CUES — What words and numbers should trigger a specific answer in nephrology questions?
cards.lecturer: Summary of the discriminating features and figures drawn across the whole lecture.
cards.explain:
## ANATOMY cues

**"Retroperitoneal, T12–L3"** · **"renal arteries at L2"** · **"Gerota's fascia"**

>> **The LEFT kidney is more SUPERIOR** (the liver pushes the right down) → **so the LEFT kidney is usually biopsied**

>> **Kidneys receive 20% of CARDIAC OUTPUT** → why they are so vulnerable to hypotension and to every circulating toxin

**Sympathetic T8–L1 → vasoconstriction · Parasympathetic vagus → vasodilation**

**Cortex holds:** corpuscle, PCT, DCT · **Medulla holds:** loop of Henle, collecting duct

>> **Glomeruli are in the CORTEX → so the biopsy needle must reach the cortex**

---

## THE 7 FUNCTIONS — and what their loss causes

| Function | Loss causes |
|---|---|
| Filtration/excretion | **Uraemia** |
| Fluid and electrolyte balance | **Oedema, hyperkalaemia** |
| Blood pressure regulation | **Hypertension** |
| Endocrine function | — |
| Acid-base homeostasis | **Acidosis → poor GROWTH** |
| Red cell production | **ANAEMIA** (erythropoietin) |
| Calcium and bone metabolism | **RENAL RICKETS** (vitamin D activation) |

---

## URINE COLLECTION cues — the thresholds

| Method | Significant |
|---|---|
| **SPA** | **ANY growth** — most reliable, **useful under 2 years** |
| **CSU (catheter)** | **≥ 10⁴ CFU/mL** |
| **MSU** | **≥ 10⁵ CFU/mL** |

>> **The dirtier the route, the higher the threshold.**

**"Bagging"** → **HIGH contamination risk** → valuable only for its **NEGATIVE predictive value**

**"Asymptomatic, no pyuria"** → **COLONIZATION, not infection**

**"Urinary frequency"** → decreased bladder incubation time → **UTI possible at LOW colony counts**

**"Examine fresh / store at 4 °C"** → delay causes **cell disintegration AND bacterial overgrowth**

---

## DIPSTICK PROTEIN — memorise

| | mg/dL |
|---|---|
| **Trace** | **< 30** |
| **+** | **30** |
| **2+** | **100** |
| **3+** | **300** |
| **4+** | **> 1000** |

>> **FALSE NEGATIVE:** **dilute** or **very acidic** urine · other proteins

>> **FALSE POSITIVE:** **concentrated** or **alkaline** urine · **gross haematuria** · **delay in reading** · contamination

>> **Memory: acid and dilute HIDE it · alkaline and concentrated FAKE it**

---

## GFR cues

**The 3 criteria for an ideal marker:** freely filtered · stable plasma concentration · **NOT reabsorbed, secreted or metabolized**

**"Creatinine"** → **SECRETED in the proximal tubule** → **OVERestimates GFR**; also affected by **age, sex, height and dietary protein**

**"Urea"** → **REABSORBED** → underestimates GFR; affected by **dietary protein and HYDRATION** → rises disproportionately in **pre-renal AKI**

>> **SCHWARTZ FORMULA: GFR = 0.413 × height (cm) ÷ serum creatinine (mg/dL)**

>> **Per 1.73 m² = the average BSA of an ideal adult** — so a child can be compared with an adult

---

## FENa — the pre-renal vs ATN test

>> **FENa = (UNa × Pcr) / (PNa × Ucr) × 100**

| FENa | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **≤ 1** | **Normovolaemia or PRE-RENAL AKI** — tubules working |
| **> 1** | **ATN** — impaired tubular transport |

>> **Low FENa = healthy tubules gripping sodium. High FENa = damaged tubules leaking it.**

**Other tubular tests:** water deprivation → **central vs nephrogenic DI** · diluting ability → **unexplained hyponatraemia** · urine acidification → **ammonium chloride challenge**

---

## IMAGING — choose by the question

**"Anything, first, in a child"** → **ULTRASOUND** — no radiation, real time, cheap, portable

>> **USS demerits: cannot see through bone (posterior acoustic shadow) · gas distorts · user dependent · depth limitation**

---

**"Nephrocalcinosis, radio-opaque stones"** → **PLAIN FILM** (declining because of USS)

**"Calyces and ureters, duplex kidney, malrotation, site of obstruction"** → **IVU**

>> **IVU NOT used in neonates, young infants or RENAL FAILURE** — it depends on the kidney excreting the contrast

>> **IVU has LOW SENSITIVITY for renal scars**

---

**"VUR or PUV"** → **VCUG — the GOLD STANDARD**

>> **Films during INSTILLATION, VOIDING and POST-VOIDING** — the **voiding** films make the diagnosis

>> **VCUG indications:** suspected ureteric dilatation · small contracted kidneys · renal failure of indeterminate cause · **terminal haematuria** · voiding difficulties · **thick walled bladder on US**

>> **VCUG demerits: high radiation · risk of UTI**

---

**"DIFFERENTIAL renal function"** → **DYNAMIC renography — Tc-DTPA or Tc-MAG3**

>> Demerit: **poor cortex visualization; cannot assess renal SIZE or MORPHOLOGY**

**"Renal SCARS · ectopic/absent kidney · acute pyelonephritis parenchymal involvement"** → **STATIC renal scan**

**"Fine calcification · Wilms tumour"** → **CT** (expensive, considerable radiation)

**"Pelvic floor detail · complex disorders · cystic lesions"** → **MRI** (no radiation, but **HEAVY SEDATION needed**)

---

## BIOPSY cues

**BIOPSY nephrotic syndrome if:** **age < 1 or > 7** · **SRNS (steroid-RESISTANT)** · **evidence of nephritis** (hypertension, haematuria, low C3, or impaired function persisting **despite volume correction**)

**DO NOT BIOPSY:** isolated microscopic glomerular haematuria · isolated non-nephrotic haematuria · **nephrotic syndrome aged 1–7** · **FREQUENTLY RELAPSING** nephrotic syndrome

>> **RELAPSING = no biopsy. RESISTANT = biopsy.**

**"Course not typical of PSAGN"** → **biopsy**

**"CKD"** → biopsy only **when kidneys are NOT shrunken**

>> **Fewer than 5 glomeruli may not be accurate** — especially in **focal** lesions

---

## BIOPSY SAFETY — the numbers

| Test | Requirement |
|---|---|
| **PCV** | **> 24–30%** |
| **Platelets** | **≥ 100,000/mm³** |
| **INR** | **< 1.2** |
| **APTT** | **< 2× control or < 40 s** |

>> **No aspirin or NSAID for 7 DAYS** (platelets are irreversibly inhibited and live 7–10 days)

>> **UNCORRECTABLE BLEEDING DIATHESIS = the ABSOLUTE contraindication**

**Also contraindicated:** uncontrolled hypertension · active renal/peri-renal infection · **hydronephrosis** · obesity · ascites · **small shrunken kidneys** · **solitary, ectopic or horseshoe kidney** · uncooperative patient · skin infection at site · no skilled operator or pathology support

**Haemodialysis** → wait **24 hours** after the last session (heparin)

**Prolonged bleeding time** → **desmopressin 0.3 µg/kg IV 30 min before**, or **DDAVP 2–4 µg/kg intranasally 2 hours before**

---

## PROCEDURE cues

**PRONE** for native kidney (retroperitoneal) · **SUPINE** for graft (iliac fossa) · **lateral decubitus** if ascites

**LEFT kidney · LOWER POLE · CORTEX · real-time USS · 16G or 18G · 2–3 passes**

**Sedation:** **midazolam 0.1 mg/kg + ketamine 0.5–1.0 mg/kg** · ± atropine 0.01 mg/kg · **NPO 4 hours** with **5% D/W**

**Post-biopsy monitoring:** **¼ hourly × 1 hr → half hourly × 2 hr → hourly × 4 hr → 4 hourly × 24 hr**

---

## TRANSPORT MEDIA — a classic question

| Examination | Medium |
|---|---|
| **Light microscopy** | **10% buffered formalin** |
| **Immunofluorescence** | **Michel's transport media** |
| **Electron microscopy** | **GLUTARALDEHYDE** |

---

## COMPLICATIONS of biopsy

**Significant haematuria · anaemia and hypotension · bowel or solid organ perforation · LOSS OF A KIDNEY · DEATH · AV malformation · hypertension · persistent loin pain**

---

## URODYNAMICS

**NON-INVASIVE:** **uroflowmetry** (urine speed and volume) · **post-void residual urine**

**INVASIVE:** cystometry · pressure flow study · **electromyography**

**Used in:** neurogenic bladder · incontinence · voiding dysfunction · **VUR** · anorectal malformations
cards.tie:
This is your fast-recall layer.

And the principle running through the whole lecture:

>> **Urine and biopsy investigate the FILTER. Imaging investigates the PLUMBING. Isotope scans investigate FUNCTION.**
